# Conroe ISD Board Meeting — 2024-07-16

# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe Independent School District Board of Trustees
### Regular Meeting — July 16, 2024

*These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript and may contain errors or omissions.*

---

## Opening
- Meeting called to order at 6:01 PM by President Hubert with a quorum present.
- Invocation by Trustee Dayron Williams; Pledge of Allegiance led by Trustee Tiffany Nelson.

---

## Special Recognitions

### Destination Imagination Global Champions
- **Presenter:** Chris Reichelt
- Conroe ISD had **146 teams** participate in Destination Imagination during the 2023–2024 school year.
- **24 teams** advanced to state; **15 teams** advanced to Global Finals in Kansas City.
- **Two teams won first place at Global Finals (world champions):**
  - **DI Veterans from Vogal Intermediate** — Team Manager: Sher Cusano; Members: Garis Bailey, Haley Bahus, Alex Cusano, Chris Masa (absent), Kenzie Miller, Abby Privet, Shan Shukla (absent)
  - **Martha Team from Knox Junior High** — Team Manager: Tina Patel (second consecutive year at Globals); Members: Benjamin Zhao (absent), Ava Checkman, Hayden Patel, Kishma Pary, Maddie Dillard, Rishab Yadav
- Teams competed against delegations from around the world (Australia, South Korea, India, Norway, Switzerland, etc.).
- Reichelt thanked the board for continued support of the DI program.

### Regeneron International Science and Engineering Fair (ISEF) Winners
- **Presenter:** Dr. Deborah Creel, Headmaster, Academy of Science and Technology at College Park High School
- **Justin Wong and Victoria O** (rising seniors) won:
  - **First place** in Environmental Engineering at ISEF
  - **Second place overall** (Gordon Moore Award for Innovation) — accompanied by a **$55,000 prize**
- Their project: Using **ultrasound to filter microplastics from water**
- They earned their bid to ISEF through the **Texas State Science and Engineering Fair**
- Both students are working on a **patent** for their project
- Students credited Conroe ISD education going back to Mitchell Intermediate (6th grade) and sponsor Miss L. Aofe at AST
- President Hubert praised their accomplishments and humility

### Ambassador Awards — Transportation Department
- **Presenters:** Chris McCord (Chief Operations Officer) and Juan Melindes (Director of Transportation)
- McCord reported transportation is in its **best position in many years**, citing improved morale, professionalism, customer service, and equipment
- **Two new transportation centers** opened (Caney Creek and Conroe)
- **31 new buses** received last year; **82 new buses** scheduled to arrive November–May
  - 30 of the 82 are part of a Heavy-Duty Vehicle Grant application; if awarded, could refund nearly **$3 million**
- **Ambassador Award Recipients:**
  - **Jamie Heraldo** (Conroe Center, Bus 1021) — ensured safety of a scared 2nd grader
  - **Telicia Price** (Conroe Center, Bus 1028) — 9-year employee; excels in field trip management and training
  - **Stephanie Malone** (Conroe Center/Caney Creek router) — 10-year employee; excels in routing and mentoring
  - **Stacy Broadway** (Caney Creek, Bus 2043) — safeguarded a 1st grader when parent was absent at bus stop
  - **Caroline Co Pepper & Heather Jones** (Oakridge Center, Bus 4217) — found a firearm (later determined fake) at a congested intersection, followed safety procedures (Jones absent; Co Pepper accepted for both)
  - **Caesar Moa** (Oakridge, Bus 4062) — not present
  - **Stella Pearson** (Woodlands Center, Bus 3023) — not present
  - **John Canary** — left district but recognized for service

### Ambassador Awards — Police Department
- **Presenter:** Chief Blake Loock
- **Ambassador Award Recipients:**
  - **Douglas Stalinsky** (Prevention Control Monitor, York Junior High) — not present (on vacation); goes above and beyond for safety including directing traffic during dangerous storms
  - **Officer David Branson** — Advanced peace officer, ALERT active shooter instructor; assigned to Washington High School; participated in staff vs. student basketball game
  - **Officer Dean Beckwith** — not present; recruited from San Diego; assigned to Pete Junior High; newly certified as police instructor and ALERT trainer
  - **Dispatcher Melissa Holmes** — since 2015; master telecommunicator license; participates in community outreach; known for patience and empathy on 911 calls
  - **Crossing Guard Miranda West** — Powell Elementary and Mitchell Intermediate; crosses 200+ students daily; greets them by name; active in school events. Her mother is also a CISD crossing guard recognized two years prior.

---

## Citizen Participation (Public Comments)

**12 speakers addressed the board.** Topics included:

### School Safety/Drills
- **Gwen Melin** asked about drill compliance tracking at Vogal Intermediate, stating attempts to get information from campus administrators were unsuccessful. Administration committed to having someone reach out.

### Books/Library Materials
- **Alexander Harris** read an explicit excerpt from *The Color Purple* (on the AP list), arguing it is inappropriate for minors. Criticized specific trustees for their positions on the book/materials policy.
- **Audrey Warner** urged the board to strengthen the materials policy to make it harder for inappropriate content to enter schools and harder for removed materials to be reinstated.
- **Susan Scrubs** noted 19 out of 5,000 instructional books were removed by a committee; argued this is not "book banning." Cited *After the Ink Dries* as an example of inappropriate content still on library shelves. Thanked Trustees Odin, Weller, Nelson, and Dungan for protecting children.

### Title IX / Transgender Issues
- **Lindsay Dawson** supported Trustee Nelson's previous resolution against new Title IX changes. Noted a federal judge issued a **preliminary injunction on July 11** prohibiting enforcement of the new Title IX rule in Texas starting August 1. Also noted **HB 3908** (fentanyl prevention training, 2023) has not been implemented. Criticized Trustees Chase and Williams.
- **Carolyn Nene** cited a 5,000% increase in transgender ideology, referenced the American College of Pediatrics statement against transgender treatment for children, and the Texas Supreme Court's 8-1 decision upholding the state's ban on gender-affirming care for minors.
- **Robert Leteel** made comments opposing transgender accommodations in schools.
- **Deborah Ferris** thanked conservative board members; shared a personal story about a great-niece who identified as transgender briefly but later changed her mind; urged the board to keep sexuality discussions out of schools.

### Teacher/Parent Rights
- **Reena Beetle** (former Conroe ISD teacher, Grangerland Intermediate) stated that in a 2021 Zoom meeting, teachers were told they must call students by their preferred name/gender and could not inform parents. She said she raised concerns with administrators who confirmed the directive came from the district. She asked the board to create a policy supporting Senate Bill 11 (parental rights).

### General Comments
- **Robert Holden** made wide-ranging critical comments about DEI, transgender policies, and board performance.
- **Diane Daniel** urged the board to focus on basics (reading, writing, arithmetic), communication skills, and respect; criticized the board for various perceived failures.
- **Paul Laskoski** reviewed 11 years of board meetings on YouTube; praised Dr. Null, staff, and Trustees Wagman, Chase, and Williams; criticized the "mama bears" group for doubling meeting durations since 2022 with discussions he deemed unproductive.
- **Cassandra Crow** (staff for State Rep. Steve Toth) announced a Texas House resolution honoring a 12-year-old Conroe ISD student (rising 9th grader at Grand Oaks who skipped two grades); details to come when the student can be present. Called for unity and community spirit.

### Incident During Public Comments
- **Trustee Nelson** stated on the record that **Trustee Williams** said "sit your ass down" under his breath to a speaker during public comments, calling it "completely inappropriate."

---

## Consent Agenda

- **Item D** (Professional Development Plan) was **extracted** from the consent agenda for separate discussion.
- **Remaining consent items approved.**
  - **Vote:** Passed (one "no" vote noted during voice vote, though the member then clarified they were "for" it)

### Item D — Professional Development Plan (2024–2025)
- Extracted at the request of Trustee Nelson for clarification.
- Dr. Null explained this item covers **required SBOE annual trainings** (e.g., pest management, sexual harassment, cybersecurity), not the full staff development plan.
- Key point: Multiple trainings contain **Title IX components**. Given the recent federal judge's ruling blocking new Title IX rules, the district will **revert to the 2020 versions** of affected trainings, ensuring none of the new Title IX tenets are included.
- **Vote: Approved unanimously** (by show of hands)

---

## Hurricane Beryl Impact and Response (Information Item)

- **Presenters:** Dr. Null, Chris McCord, Karen Garza, Marshall Shrader
- Hurricane Beryl hit **July 8, 2024**. McCord had flagged it to Dr. Null on **June 29**.
- **Key points:**
  - Maintenance team was out the same day the hurricane hit, restoring buildings even as their own homes lacked power
  - **Supply distribution** coordinated with Montgomery County and Food Bank at Woodforest Bank Stadium, Grand Oaks HS, and Caney Creek HS
  - **Anti-scaling fencing** (Chapter 61 compliance) sustained significant damage at multiple locations; repair is underway
  - **Electrical outages** damaged HVAC systems including chillers and air handlers
  - **Communication challenges** — cell service was out ("SOS for days"); higher-powered radios were used by key personnel
  - **Debris/tree cleanup** costs expected to be "expensive and significant"
  - **Construction timeline** lost one week on already tight schedules
  - Transportation and maintenance departments provided **diesel to county ambulances** during the crisis
  - The **Jet Center** served as a rally point; the **Network Operations Center (NOC)** performed flawlessly on generators
  - **Payroll** was processed on time thanks to Karen Garza's team and Rachel Jimenez working in the building without power/AC
  - A church displaced from Burnham Woods (no power) was relocated to Bradley for Sunday services

- **Financial Impact:**
  - Could exceed **$1 million** in losses
  - Property insurance has a **2% deductible per location** (wind claim) — unlikely to recover anything
  - District monitoring for possible **FEMA assistance**
  - For FY 2025–26, the board could consider **disaster pennies** (additional golden pennies allowed by tax code after a federally declared disaster) — could generate approximately **$24 million** — board approval only, no voter approval required, one-year duration
  - The district used disaster pennies once before during **Hurricane Harvey**

- **Discussion on fencing:** Trustee Nelson raised concerns about recurring damage to anti-scaling fencing (particularly at York JH), the ongoing cost, and whether grant funds can be used for repairs (currently grants allow installation but not repair). Discussion about possibly using 8-foot non-slatted fencing in high-wind areas.

---

## Student Code of Conduct (2024–2025)
- **Presenter:** Dr. Medford
- Changes: Removed reference to behavior coordinators; added **nicotine pouches** to prohibited items; defined **electronic devices** in glossary
- No legislative or case law changes warranting other modifications
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

---

## Updated Outside Facility Use Fee Schedule
- **Presenter:** Chris McCord
- Facility rental rates last updated in **2016** (effective January 2017)
- Internal audit (July 2022) recommended cost recovery increase based on CPI (up 22% from Jan 2017 to July 2022, plus another 6.1% since)
- Nearly **800 general rentals** between August 2023 and May 2024
- District was running a **negative delta of ~$50,000** (not recovering costs)
- **Key recommendations:**
  - General rate increases averaging ~20%, with some categories (e.g., auditoriums) seeing larger increases
  - **Classrooms will no longer be rented** (only one church currently rents a classroom; their lease ends in December)
  - Staff support fees increased ~20%
  - New athletic facility rental rates established (including reduced rates for employee-sponsored camps/clinics per board policy GKD)
  - Jet Center rental rates established (though rarely rented due to heavy district use)
  - Rates include custodian and supplies
  - **Effective January 1, 2025** (to give organizations time to plan)
- Expected to generate an additional **$200,000–$250,000** in revenue
- Rates compared with Tomball, Klein, Spring, and Humble ISDs
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

---

## TEA Waivers (Items 6A–6E)
All five waivers were presented by Dr. Medford and approved unanimously.

### 6A — Low Attendance Waiver
- For campuses where attendance fell 10+ percentage points below prior year average due to the **April 8 total solar eclipse**
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

### 6B — Missed School Day Waiver
- Patterson, Bosman, and Stockton closed **May 17, 2024**; Broadway Elementary closed **May 22, 2024** due to prolonged power outage from severe weather
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

### 6C — Teacher Data Portal / Texas Assessment Management System Waiver
- Allows use of local system (**Edoria**) instead of state system for assessments, appraisals, and certification tracking
- **Three-year waiver** (2024–25 through 2026–27); continuation of existing practice
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

### 6D — Modified Schedule for State Assessment Testing Days Waiver
- Allows non-testing upper-level students late arrival on testing days without attendance penalty
- Through 2026–27 school year; continuation of existing practice
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

### 6E — Foreign Exchange Student Waiver
- Limits foreign exchange students to **six or fewer per high school** due to capacity issues
- **Three-year waiver** through 2026–27; continuation of existing practice
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

---

| Project | Status |
|---------|--------|
| **Safety & Security Program (2019 Bond final installment)** | On schedule; inspections disrupted by hurricane but resumed; expected completion end of 2024 |
| **Bartlett Elementary** | Substantially complete; scheduled to open August 2024; learning materials arriving this week |
| **Isolde Elementary (Flex School #24)** | On schedule for August 2025 opening; detention ponds and foundations under construction |
| **Campbell Elementary (Flex School #25)** | On schedule for August 2025; slightly ahead in construction |
| **Grand Oaks 7-8-9 Grade Campus** | On schedule for January 2027 opening; building pad ~50% complete; developer constructing surrounding roads |
| **Campus Renovations (4 campuses)** | Affected by Hurricane Beryl (lost a few days); AC coming on this week; ceiling grids going back in; freezer/cooler delayed at Lamar but rental equipment in place; will not affect first day of school |
| **Conroe High School (phased construction)** | Fine Arts wings turned over before July 4; band students moving in; new dining courtyard turfed; kitchen floor finish delayed by power loss — working on contingency plan with Child Nutrition; "Tower" (3-story section) demolition nearly complete; Phase 3 (new front door) scheduled for completion December 2025 |

## No Red Ink Purchase Authorization
- Request to add **No Red Ink** (premium student licensing) to the over-$50K vendor list for FY 2023–24
- Required by local board policy CH
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

---

## 2024–2025 Preliminary Budget Update (Information Item)
- **Presenter:** Karen Garza, CFO

### Key Budget Highlights:
- **Projected deficit improved** from $17.7M (April estimate) to **$11.9M** — improvement of $5.8M
  - Improvement driven by: modified state calculations for SB 12 hold harmless (over-65 tax ceiling recalculation), and full year of attendance data
- **Student enrollment growth:** ~1,500 projected
- **Certified property value growth:** Estimated at **8.5%**, reaching **$55.5 billion**
- **Projected tax rate:** Overall **decrease of ~1.5 cents** (despite voter-approved 2-cent increase from 2023 bond) due to 3.5 cents of M&O compression; I&S increases from 26 to 28 cents as voter-approved
- **Tax rate history:** Decreased **26.56 cents** since 2021

### Revenue & Expenditures:
- Total estimated revenue: **~$700 million**
- Total estimated expenditures: **$711.97 million**
- **Estimated budget shortfall: $11.92 million**

### Personnel:
- **147 new FTE positions** at $10.8M cost
- Over half (**70.5 FTE / $5.6M**) for special education
- Special education student count grew from 7,230 (2023) to 9,680 (June 2024) — increase of ~2,450; projected to grow another 1,500
- No new district-level support positions added

### Fund Balance:
- Target: 20–25% of budget (~$142.5M)
- Projected unassigned fund balance at end of FY 2023–24: **$152.23M**
- After $11.9M deficit: **~$140.3M (19.7%)** — below the 20% target by ~$2.1M

### Looking Ahead to FY 2025–26:
- Projected deficit of **$17.43 million** (including $12M for new campus staffing, but no pay raises)
- ~225 positions equivalent to the deficit amount
- Administration will begin developing a **"menu" of potential cuts** internally this fall
  - Will involve campus principals in prioritization
  - Will present to the board in **December/January**
  - Will consider ease of adding positions back if state funding materializes
  - Special program compliance spending requirements must be maintained
  - Goal: Assure all current employees they will have jobs (though positions may change)

### Budget Strategy Discussion:
- **Disaster pennies** could generate ~$24M (two pennies) — board approval only, one year
  - Could be used to balance the budget and replenish fund balance
  - Could be combined with a future VATRE (Voter-Approved Tax Rate Election) in November 2025 or 2026 for perpetual access
- **VATRE** would require efficiency audit by July 5 and November election
- The November 2023 pay raise (~$15M) is the primary driver of the current deficit position
- **Esser funds** did NOT contribute to the deficit — all Esser-funded positions were absorbed into regular formulas
- ADA percentage: Ended year at ~92.4% (budget assumes 92.5%); 1% = $4.5M impact
- Bond rating agencies are aware of statewide deficit trends; as long as the district has a plan, ratings should not be affected

## Tax Assessor-Collector Resolution
- Resolution designating **Tammy McCrae** (Montgomery County Tax Assessor-Collector) to calculate truth-in-taxation requirements
- Continuation of existing practice; now required to be formally designated
- **Vote: Approved unanimously**

## Financial Statements (as of June 30, 2024)
- **Presenter:** Karen Garza
- General Fund assets: $291.52M
- Year-to-date revenue: $554M; Expenditures: $491.3M
- 2023 Bond: $274.5M encumbered/expended life-to-date; $1.7M remaining
- Self-funded insurance: June loss of $63,100; year-to-date surplus of $1.6M (averaging $167K/month surplus)
- Investments: Par value $996.5M; portfolio yield 5.37%; benchmark (90-day T-bill) 5.2%
